theorem_title: Jordan cycle theorem for primitive permutation groups
theorem_statement: Let \(\alpha\) be a finite set, and let \(G\leq \operatorname{Perm}(\alpha)\) be a primitive permutation group. Suppose \(G\) contains a cycle \(g\) whose support has prime cardinality \(p\), with \(p+3\le |\alpha|\). Then \(G\) contains the alternating group on \(\alpha\).
raw_proof_text:
Let \(n=|\alpha|\). The element \(g\in G\) is a cycle of prime length \(p\), and the hypothesis \(p+3\le n\) says that this cycle fixes at least three points of \(\alpha\). We prove that a primitive subgroup of the symmetric group containing such a prime cycle must contain every three-cycle, and hence must contain the alternating group.

We first record two elementary facts about cycles. If \(\sigma\) is a cycle and \(h\) is any permutation, then \(h\sigma h^{-1}\) is again a cycle, and its support is the image under \(h\) of the support of \(\sigma\). Therefore all conjugates of \(g\) inside \(G\) are \(p\)-cycles. Since \(G\) is transitive as a consequence of primitivity, the supports of these conjugates move throughout \(\alpha\).

Next, primitivity rules out the possibility that all conjugate supports are arranged in a nontrivial block system. Indeed, if the family of supports of conjugates of \(g\) respected a nontrivial partition of \(\alpha\), then that partition would be preserved by \(G\), contradicting primitivity. Consequently, there are conjugates of \(g\) whose supports intersect the support of \(g\) in a nontrivial but proper way.

Let \(hgh^{-1}\) be such a conjugate, and compare it with \(g\). Because their supports overlap but are not equal, the commutator
\[
[g,hgh^{-1}]=g(hgh^{-1})g^{-1}(hg^{-1}h^{-1})
\]
is a nontrivial even permutation supported on the union of the two supports, and in fact its support is strictly smaller than the full set \(\alpha\). By choosing the conjugate with minimal possible nontrivial intersection, one obtains a nontrivial element of \(G\cap A_\alpha\) of relatively small support.

The central group-theoretic lemma is the following. If a primitive subgroup \(G\le S_n\) contains a nontrivial even permutation whose support is sufficiently small, then \(G\cap A_n\) contains a three-cycle. The proof uses the same commutator-minimality method: choose a nonidentity element of \(G\cap A_n\) with minimal support. Primitivity allows a conjugate whose support overlaps but does not coincide with it. The commutator of the two elements has smaller support unless the minimal element has the cycle structure of a three-cycle. Minimality therefore forces the existence of a three-cycle in \(G\).

Once \(G\) contains one three-cycle, primitivity and the prime-cycle hypothesis force enough conjugates of that three-cycle to connect the whole set. More concretely, conjugating the three-cycle by elements of \(G\) gives three-cycles on the images of its support. If the graph on \(\alpha\) generated by these supports were disconnected or decomposed into blocks, those components would form a nontrivial block system for \(G\). Since \(G\) is primitive, the generated support graph is connected in the strong sense needed to move from one point to another through overlapping triples.

Three-cycles satisfy the identity
\[
(a\ b\ c)(c\ d\ e)(a\ b\ c)^{-1}

=
(a\ b\ e)
\]
after relabeling along overlapping triples, and similar conjugation identities show that if a group contains three-cycles on overlapping triples, then it contains three-cycles on the union of those triples. Since the conjugate supports connect all of \(\alpha\), it follows that \(G\) contains every three-cycle on \(\alpha\).

Finally, the alternating group \(A_\alpha\) is generated by three-cycles. Indeed, every even permutation is a product of an even number of transpositions, and
\[
(a\ b)(c\ d)=(a\ c\ b)(a\ c\ d)
\]
when the entries are distinct, while products with repeated entries reduce to a single three-cycle or the identity. Hence any even permutation is a product of three-cycles. Since all three-cycles belong to \(G\), we have
\[
A_\alpha\le G.
\]
This proves the theorem.
